text.title: Regression Test First Bugfix Workflow — Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every application of `regression-test-first-bugfix-workflow` must enforce. The rules pin the discipline that turns a method from folklore into a reviewable, owned, version-controlled operating tool.

rule.statement: The methodology fires on a named trigger (event, threshold, schedule). Vague triggers ('when needed') are rejected.

rationale.source: Operating-system / cron design patterns; trigger-action behavioural research

rule.rationale: Methodologies without explicit triggers either fire constantly or never. A named trigger makes the cadence enforceable.

rule.statement: Output shape is fixed (template, schema, or artefact path). Free-form output is rejected at review.
rule.rationale: Methodologies produce reusable assets only when their output is shaped. Free-form output is one-off and dies on completion.

rule.statement: Every conclusion ties to evidence (data, ticket, transcript). Unevidenced conclusions are demoted to hypotheses.
rule.rationale: TDD methodology exists but not as 'red-test-from-prod-bug' workflow. This is the most common testing situation for an FE/BE dev on a real product.

rule.statement: Every instance has a named owner accountable for the next-step actions. Outputs without owners are tagged stale at the next cadence.
rule.rationale: Anonymous outputs do not produce change. The owner is the cheapest mechanism that connects artefact to behaviour.

rule.statement: The methodology is reviewed against outcomes at a published cadence (monthly or quarterly). Methodologies that produce no measurable change are deprecated.
rule.rationale: Methodologies without an outcome review become folklore. The review loop is what keeps them honest.
